name: reducing-entropy
description: Manual-only skill for minimizing total codebase size. Only activate when explicitly requested by user. Measures success by final code amount, not effort. Bias toward deletion.
capabilities: Evaluates changes by measuring total code before and after
Applies three key questions to assess every proposed modification
Loads philosophical mindsets (data-over-abstractions, simpler-is-better) to guide simplification
Detects anti-patterns that justify adding code (flexibility, separation of concerns)
Measures success by final codebase size, not effort expended
author: softaworks
securitySummary: This is a manual-only documentation skill with no scripts, no executable code, and no network capabilities. All static scanner findings are false positives triggered by markdown documentation patterns (code spans, external reference links, YAML frontmatter). The skill only contains philosophical guidance for minimizing codebase size.
valueStatement: Codebases naturally accumulate complexity over time, leading to more bugs and maintenance burden. This skill provides a framework for evaluating every change based on whether it reduces total code in the final codebase.
limitations: Manual-only skill that requires explicit user activation
Does not automatically refactor code - provides guidance only
Does not execute commands or modify files directly
Does not work well with frameworks that have strong conventions

useCases.title: Refactoring Legacy Code
useCases.description: When cleaning up technical debt in an existing codebase, use this skill to evaluate whether proposed refactoring actually reduces total code or just reorganizes complexity.

useCases.title: Feature Request Evaluation
useCases.description: When deciding whether to add a new feature, this skill helps assess if the benefit justifies the code cost and identifies opportunities to remove existing code.

useCases.title: Code Review Simplification
useCases.description: During code reviews, use this skill to challenge proposals that add abstractions, layers, or flexibility that may not be immediately necessary.

promptTemplates.title: Beginner: Simple Refactor Request
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to add validation to my user registration form. Help me implement this using the reducing-entropy skill. First, load a relevant mindset from the references directory and explain its core principle.
promptTemplates.scenario: Adding a new feature with focus on minimal code

promptTemplates.title: Intermediate: Feature Comparison
promptTemplates.prompt: Compare two implementation approaches for adding search functionality: Approach A adds a new search service with 200 lines, Approach B adds 30 lines to existing controllers. Which approach reduces entropy? Load the appropriate mindset and apply the three questions.

promptTemplates.title: Advanced: Full Codebase Review
promptTemplates.prompt: Review my authentication module (files: auth.js, token.js, session.js, refresh.js). For each file, identify what can be deleted, merged, or simplified. Apply the 'worse-is-better' mindset and explain how each change affects total code count.

promptTemplates.title: Expert: Architectural Decision
promptTemplates.prompt: Our team wants to add a caching layer between our API and database. Evaluate this decision through the reducing-entropy lens. Load the 'essential-vs-accidental' mindset and the 'locality-of-behavior' mindset. What questions should we ask? What code might become obsolete?

bestPractices: Always load at least one mindset from the references directory before applying this skill - the philosophical grounding helps make better trade-offs
Count actual lines before and after every change - measure the end state, not the effort
When tempted to add flexibility, ask 'for what?' - YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It) often applies
antiPatterns: Using 'cleaner architecture' as justification to add code - organization does not reduce total lines
Accepting 'more maintainable code' without quantifying the maintenance cost savings
Avoiding churn by keeping existing code when deleting it would reduce entropy

faq.question: What is entropy in a codebase?
faq.answer: Entropy is the natural tendency of codebases to grow more complex over time. Each feature, abstraction, and layer adds to the total code that must be understood, tested, and maintained. This skill fights that tendency by biasing toward deletion.

faq.question: How is this different from standard refactoring?
faq.answer: Standard refactoring often focuses on code organization and readability within existing functionality. This skill specifically measures total code reduction - reorganization that adds lines is rejected even if it improves structure.

faq.question: When should I not use this skill?
faq.answer: Do not use this skill when the codebase is already minimal for what it does, when working in frameworks with strong conventions that you should not fight, or when regulatory requirements mandate specific structures.

faq.question: What are the reference mindsets?
faq.answer: Reference mindsets are philosophical frameworks that provide deeper grounding for simplification decisions. Examples include 'data-over-abstractions', 'essential-vs-accidental', 'worse-is-better', and 'locality-of-behavior'. You must load at least one before applying the skill.

faq.question: Does this skill write code?
faq.answer: No. This is a manual-only skill that provides guidance and evaluation criteria. It does not execute commands or directly modify files. It helps you make better decisions about what code to add, delete, or keep.

faq.question: How do I measure success with this skill?
faq.answer: Success is measured by the final codebase size - fewer lines, fewer files, fewer functions. Writing 50 lines that delete 200 lines is a win. Adding abstractions that increase total code is a loss, regardless of how organized they make the code.
